_meta: Phase C draft — human commitment judgments. Covers Sections 6-18 (D1 through aporia). Preamble sections (1-5) contain no definitional commitments. Schema v3.0, five-type taxonomy.
_finding: conditional_commitment has zero instances in the Euthyphro. Every apparent conditional is actually a derivation from independently conceded premises. Type retained in schema for other dialogues.

entries.proposition: Piety is prosecuting anyone guilty of murder, sacrilege, or similar crimes, regardless of relation
entries.note: Euthyphro volunteers this as D1. It is an assertion, not a commitment, because Socrates has not yet bound him to it under questioning — he offered it freely.

entries.proposition: Not prosecuting the guilty is impiety
entries.note: Contrapositive of C-6.1, volunteered in the same speech.

entries.proposition: The actions of the gods (Zeus punishing Kronos) provide moral precedent for human conduct
entries.note: Euthyphro appeals to Zeus/Kronos as justification. The presupposition that divine action is paradigmatic is not challenged here but is available for exploitation. Socrates declines to challenge it (T42-T44), saving it.

sections.title: Refutation of D1: example is not definition

entries.proposition: There are many pious acts besides prosecuting one's father
entries.note: Socrates asks 'you would admit that there are many other pious acts?' Euthyphro: 'There are.' Extracted under questioning.

entries.proposition: There is one idea (eidos) that makes all pious things pious
entries.note: Socrates: 'Do you not recollect that there was one idea which made the impious impious, and the pious pious?' Euthyphro: 'I remember.' Ratified under questioning.

entries.proposition: D1 fails: an example of a pious act is not the general idea (eidos) of piety

_if_derivation.conclusion: D1 (piety = prosecuting wrongdoers) gives an example, not a definition. The eidos requirement is unmet.

entries.note: Euthyphro does not object. He proceeds to offer D2, implicitly accepting that D1 was inadequate.

removed.note: D1 is superseded by D2, not formally retracted. Euthyphro never says 'I was wrong about D1.'

sections.title: Definition 2: what is dear to the gods

entries.proposition: Piety is what is dear to the gods
entries.note: Euthyphro: 'Piety, then, is that which is dear to the gods.' This is D2. Volunteered but in response to Socratic demand for a definition, so it functions as a commitment — Socrates treats it as binding.

entries.proposition: Impiety is what is not dear to the gods
entries.note: Contrapositive of C-8.1, stated in the same turn.

entries.proposition: The gods have enmities, hatreds, and differences
entries.note: Socrates: 'the gods were admitted to have enmities and hatreds and differences?' Euthyphro: 'Yes, that was also said.' Re-affirming a prior concession.

entries.proposition: Disputes about the just, the good, and the honorable cannot be resolved by measurement
entries.note: Socrates proposes this; Euthyphro: 'Yes, Socrates, the nature of the differences about which we quarrel is such as you describe.'

entries.proposition: The gods quarrel about the just, the good, and the honorable
entries.note: Socrates: 'And the quarrels of the gods, noble Euthyphro, when they occur, are of a like nature?' Euthyphro: 'Certainly they are.' Application of C-9.2 to the divine case.

entries.proposition: Every agent loves what it deems noble and just, and hates the opposite
entries.note: General principle. Socrates: 'Does not every man love that which he deems noble and just and good, and hate the opposite of them?' Euthyphro: 'Very true.'

entries.proposition: The same things are regarded as just by some and unjust by others
entries.note: Euthyphro: 'Very true.' Conceding that value judgments diverge.

entries.proposition: The same things are both hated and loved by the gods

_if_derivation.conclusion: The same things are hated by the gods and loved by the gods, and are both hateful and dear to them.

entries.note: Socrates: 'Then the same things are hated by the gods and loved by the gods?' Euthyphro: 'True.' Derivation ratified — available to both.

entries.proposition: Under D2, the same things are both pious and impious

_if_derivation.conclusion: Under D2 (piety = dear to gods, impiety = not dear to gods), the same things will be both pious and impious.

entries.note: Euthyphro: 'So I should suppose.' The hedge ('I should suppose') is weaker than his usual 'True' or 'Certainly' — he is beginning to see the problem but has not fully conceded. Marked medium confidence.

removed.note: D2 produces contradiction via D-9.2. Not formally retracted by Euthyphro — he moves to D3 (the 'all gods' amendment).

sections.title: Amendment: what ALL the gods love

entries.proposition: All the gods agree that a murderer should be punished
entries.note: Euthyphro objects to D2's refutation by asserting divine unanimity on murder. This is an assertion (volunteered as a defense), not a commitment (not extracted). Socrates does not accept it — he presses on the particular case. Confidence medium because the assertion is strategic, not philosophical.

entries.proposition: Gods and men disagree about who the wrongdoer is in specific cases, not about whether wrongdoers should be punished
entries.note: Socrates draws this out through T86-T94. Euthyphro: 'That is true, Socrates, in the main.' The distinction between general principle and particular application.

sections.title: Definition 3: what all the gods love

entries.proposition: Piety is what all the gods love
entries.note: D3. Euthyphro: 'Yes, I should say that what all the gods love is pious and holy.' Adopts Socrates' amendment (T102). Note: this is DIFFERENT from C-8.1 ('dear to the gods' vs 'all the gods love') — semantic distinction preserved per normalization rule 4.

entries.proposition: Impiety is what all the gods hate
entries.note: Contrapositive of C-11.1.

entries.proposition: The definition 'piety is what all the gods love' will stand the test of enquiry
entries.note: Socrates: 'Ought we to enquire into the truth of this?' Euthyphro: 'We should enquire; and I believe that the statement will stand the test.' Commits to the definition being defensible.

sections.title: Refutation of D3: the Euthyphro dilemma

entries.proposition: Any state of action or passion implies previous action or passion (the state follows the act, not the reverse)
entries.note: General principle established through carrying/being-carried, seeing/being-seen analogies. Euthyphro: 'Yes' (T119). This is the modus ponens machinery.

entries.proposition: Piety is loved by the gods because it is pious (not pious because it is loved)
entries.note: The critical fork. Socrates: 'It is loved because it is holy, not holy because it is loved?' Euthyphro: 'Yes.' Euthyphro has picked a horn: the causal direction runs from piety's nature to the gods' love.

entries.proposition: What is dear to the gods is dear because it is loved by them (not loved because it is dear)
entries.note: The other direction. 'Dear to the gods' is a state that follows from the act of being loved. Euthyphro: 'Certainly.'

entries.proposition: Holy and god-loved are not the same: substituting one for the other within 'because' contexts produces contradiction. D3 does not answer the 'what is it?' question.

_if_derivation.conclusion: From C-12.2: holy things are loved because they are holy. From C-12.3: god-loved things are god-loved because they are loved. If holy = god-loved (D3/C-11.1), substitution produces: god-loved things are god-loved because they are god-loved — but C-12.3 says they are god-loved because loved (not because god-loved). Contradiction. Therefore holy ≠ god-loved; D3 fails.

entries.note: Socrates performs the derivation in monologue (T132-T138). Euthyphro does not ratify — he responds 'I really do not know, Socrates, how to express what I mean' (T139). INTERPRETIVE NOTE: The scholarly literature divides on the argument's mechanism. (1) Aristotelian interpretation (Sharvy 1972, Judson 2010): Socrates wants an explanatorily prior feature; god-lovedness is not prior to holiness, so D3 gives an attribute (pathos), not the essence (ousia). (2) Strict identity interpretation (Ebrey 2017): Socrates' substitution works because he takes Euthyphro's proposal as claiming holy = god-loved (strict identity); substitution within 'because' contexts then produces contradiction. Ebrey argues the Aristotelian interpretation faces problems justifying the substitution step (pp. 91-94) and that ousia/pathos at 11a6-b1 means 'what it is' vs 'what is true of it', not Aristotelian essence vs attribute (p. 96). This annotation uses interpretation-neutral language: the derivation shows holy ≠ god-loved via substitution contradiction. See references/ebrey_2017_identity_and_explanation.pdf.

removed.note: D3 is refuted by D-12.1 (gives attribute not essence). Euthyphro does not formally retract — he says arguments 'walk away' (T139).
C-11.3 committed to D3 standing the test. D-12.1 shows it failed.

sections.title: Definition 4: piety as part of justice / care of gods

entries.proposition: The pious is necessarily just
entries.note: Socrates: 'Is not that which is pious necessarily just?' Euthyphro: 'Yes.' (T143)

entries.proposition: Piety is a part of justice (not all justice is pious)
entries.note: Established through the fear/reverence analogy. Euthyphro: 'No, I think that you are quite right.' (T155)

entries.proposition: Piety is that part of justice which attends to the gods
entries.note: D4. Euthyphro: 'Piety or holiness, Socrates, appears to me to be that part of justice which attends to the gods.' (T159)

sections.title: Refutation of D4: care cannot improve the gods

entries.proposition: Care (therapeia) of a thing is intended for the benefit and improvement of that thing
entries.note: Established through horses/dogs/oxen analogies. Euthyphro concedes each: 'True' (T173), 'Certainly, not for their hurt' (T175), 'Of course' (T177).

entries.proposition: Pious acts do not make the gods better
entries.note: Socrates: 'Would you say that when you do a holy act you make any of the gods better?' Euthyphro: 'No, no; that was certainly not what I meant.' (T179)

entries.proposition: D4's 'care of the gods' cannot mean what care normally means, since care improves its object and the gods cannot be improved

_if_derivation.conclusion: D4 uses 'care' (therapeia) but care improves its object; the gods cannot be improved; therefore 'care' is inapplicable in its normal sense.

entries.note: Euthyphro ratifies by saying 'that was certainly not what I meant' — acknowledging the refutation. Available to both because Euthyphro understands the point.

entries.proposition: The gods are already perfect and cannot be improved
entries.note: Never explicitly stated by either party but presupposed by Euthyphro's denial at T179. If the gods could be improved, his 'No, no' would not follow.

removed.note: D4 refuted by D-15.1. Euthyphro shifts to 'service/ministration' (T183) rather than formally retracting.

entries.proposition: Piety is a kind of service (hyperetike) that servants show to their masters
entries.note: Euthyphro shifts from 'care' to 'service' after D4's refutation. This is a repair attempt, not deeply committed — he is trying alternatives.

entries.proposition: Piety is learning how to please the gods in word and deed, by prayers and sacrifices
entries.note: D5. Euthyphro: 'Let me simply say that piety or holiness is learning how to please the gods in word and deed, by prayers and sacrifices.' (T201) This is an evasion — he refuses to answer what work the gods accomplish — but it is an explicit commitment to a definition.

entries.proposition: Piety is a science of asking and giving
entries.note: Socrates reformulates D5; Euthyphro: 'You understand me capitally, Socrates.' (T207)

entries.proposition: Our gifts to the gods are tributes of honor and what pleases them
entries.note: Socrates: 'what is the meaning of gifts which are conferred by us upon the gods?' Euthyphro: 'What else, but tributes of honour; and, as I was just now saying, what pleases them?' (T219)

entries.proposition: Piety is what is dear to the gods
entries.note: Socrates: 'Piety, then, is pleasing to the gods, but not beneficial or dear to them?' Euthyphro: 'I should say that nothing could be dearer.' (T221) Euthyphro has arrived back at the language of D2/D3 without realizing it.

entries.proposition: D5 reduces to 'piety is what is dear to the gods', which is D3/D2

_if_derivation.conclusion: D5 (science of asking and giving) → giving what pleases the gods → what is dear to the gods → D3 (what all the gods love) / D2 (what is dear to the gods). The chain of reductions returns to a prior definition.

entries.note: Socrates: 'Then once more the assertion is repeated that piety is dear to the gods?' Euthyphro: 'Certainly.' (T222-T223) Euthyphro ratifies without recognizing the circularity.

removed.note: C-16.1 (service/hyperetike) is a repair attempt that Euthyphro abandons when Socrates presses on what work the gods accomplish. Superseded by C-16.2 (prayers and sacrifices) — Euthyphro shifts from the abstract 'service' to the concrete 'sacrifice and prayer' when he cannot specify the telos of service.

entries.proposition: It was previously established that piety is not the same as what is loved by the gods
entries.note: Socrates: 'Were we not saying that the holy or pious was not the same with that which is loved of the gods? Have you forgotten?' Euthyphro: 'I quite remember.' (T224-T225) Re-affirming D-12.1. IMPORTANT: This turn closes the information asymmetry. D-12.1 was available_to: socrates_only from T138 through T224. At T225, Euthyphro explicitly remembers the result, so D-12.1 transitions to available_to: both. The asymmetry held for the entirety of Sections 13-17 (D4 and D5), during which Euthyphro proposed two new definitions without recognizing that the D-12.1 derivation would eventually close the cycle.

entries.proposition: The current position is that what is loved of the gods is holy
entries.note: Socrates: 'And are you not saying that what is loved of the gods is holy; and is not this the same as what is dear to them—do you see?' Euthyphro: 'True.' (T226-T227). IMPORTANT: This turn does double duty. Socrates explicitly equates 'loved of the gods' with 'dear to them', collapsing the D2/D3 semantic distinction that normalization rule 4 preserves. This bridging is what allows C-16.5 ('dear to the gods') to reach D3 ('what all the gods love') in the cycle chain. Without this explicit equation at T226, the cycle would not close — the two propositions are different under the normalization rules.

entries.proposition: Either the earlier refutation was wrong, or the current position is wrong
entries.note: Socrates: 'Then either we were wrong in our former assertion; or, if we were right then, we are wrong now.' Euthyphro: 'One of the two must be true.' (T228-T229)

cycle_signal.via: D5 (science of asking/giving) → giving what pleases the gods (C-16.4) → what is dear to the gods (C-16.5) → Socrates equates 'dear to gods' with 'loved of gods' at T226 (C-18.2 note) → D3 (what all the gods love). D3 was refuted by D-12.1 (gives attribute not essence).
cycle_signal.bridging_step: T226: Socrates explicitly equates 'loved of the gods' with 'dear to them', collapsing the D2/D3 distinction. Without this bridging, C-16.5 and D3 are different propositions under normalization rule 4.

D1.text: Piety is prosecuting anyone guilty of wrongdoing

D2.text: Piety is what is dear to the gods

D3.text: Piety is what all the gods love
D3.refutation_note: Holy and god-loved shown not to be the same via substitution contradiction. Whether this demonstrates explanatory priority (Aristotelian) or strict non-identity (Ebrey 2017) is contested. See D-12.1 annotation note.

D4.text: Piety is that part of justice which attends to the gods

D5.text: Piety is learning how to please the gods in word and deed
